# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains controlled empirical evidence about how single-agent and multi-agent LLM systems perform across four agentic benchmarks, including measured effects for architecture, task structure, coordination overhead, error amplification, efficiency, and predictive modeling of architecture-task fit.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study evaluates 180 controlled agent-system configurations spanning 5 architectures, 3 LLM families, and 4 agentic benchmarks with matched prompts, tools, and token budgets.

## Source Excerpt

“We evaluate five canonical architectures: Single Agent System (SAS) and four Multi-Agent variants (Independent, Centralized, Decentralized, Hybrid) instantiated across three major LLM families ... on four representative agentic benchmarks ... Across N = 180 controlled configurations with matched token budgets, we derive a scaling principle...”

## Note

The source frames this design as controlling implementation confounds to isolate architectural effects.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper’s predictive model achieves cross-validated R² = 0.513 on held-out data.

## Source Excerpt

“We establish a predictive framework using empirical coordination metrics ... achieving cross-validated R2 = 0.513...”

## Note

The source presents this as variance explanation for a model built from coordination metrics and task/model properties.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Leave-one-domain-out cross-validation reaches R² = 0.89, and the framework predicts the optimal architecture for 87% of held-out task configurations.

## Source Excerpt

“Critically, this framework generalizes beyond training configurations: leave-one-domain-out cross-validation achieves R2 = 0.89, and the model correctly predicts optimal architectures for 87% of held-out task configurations...”

## Note

The source states both metrics as evidence of extrapolation to unseen task structures.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The efficiency-tools interaction is negative, with β = -0.330 and p < 0.001, indicating stronger performance penalties for multi-agent systems on tool-heavy tasks.

## Source Excerpt

“Our analysis identifies three patterns. First, a tool-coordination trade-off (β = −0.330, p < 0.001): tool-heavy tasks ... suffer from multi-agent coordination overhead...”

## Note

This evidence identifies a quantified interaction and its direction, but the source’s mechanism account remains a proposed explanation tied to fixed computational budgets and tool orchestration capacity.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
When the single-agent baseline exceeds about 45% accuracy, adding coordination shows diminishing or negative returns, with β = -0.408 and p < 0.001.

## Source Excerpt

“Second, a capability ceiling (β = −0.408, p < 0.001): tasks where single-agent performance already exceeds 45% accuracy experience negative returns from additional agents...”

## Note

This introduces a visible tension with generic “more agents help” claims because the reported benefit reverses above an empirical baseline threshold.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Independent multi-agent systems amplify errors by 17.2× relative to the single-agent baseline.

## Source Excerpt

“Third, architecture-dependent error amplification where independent multi-agent systems amplify errors 17.2-fold versus single-agent baseline through unchecked error propagation...”

## Note

This is a strong failure result for one multi-agent topology and conflicts with any assumption that parallel independent agents are automatically safer or more reliable.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Centralized coordination limits error amplification to 4.4× relative to the single-agent baseline by using an orchestrator validation bottleneck before aggregation.

## Source Excerpt

“...while centralized coordination achieves 4.4-fold containment via validation bottlenecks, where the orchestrator reviews sub-agent outputs before aggregation, catching errors before they propagate to the final response.”

## Note

The mechanism is stated directly in the source: orchestrator review acts as the containment point.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On Finance Agent, centralized multi-agent coordination improves mean performance by 80.9%, from 0.349 for the single-agent baseline to 0.631.

## Source Excerpt

“On Finance Agent, MAS achieve substantial improvements: Centralized reaches +80.9% (mean 0.631 vs. SAS 0.349)...”

## Note

The source attributes this domain to distributed financial reasoning opportunities, but this evidence unit records only the reported performance change.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On BrowseComp-Plus, decentralized coordination improves mean performance by 9.2%, from 0.318 for the single-agent baseline to 0.347.

## Source Excerpt

“On BrowseComp-Plus, improvements remain modest: Decentralized achieves +9.2% (0.347 vs. SAS 0.318)...”

## Note

The source describes this gain as modest rather than large.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
PlanCraft shows performance degradation for every multi-agent architecture tested, ranging from -39.0% to -70.0% relative to the single-agent baseline.

## Source Excerpt

“Critically, PlanCraft exhibits universal performance degradation across all multi-agent architectures. Centralized declines to -50.4% ... Decentralized to -41.4% ... Hybrid to -39.0% ... and Independent to -70.0%...”

## Note

This is a direct counterexample to uniform multi-agent benefit; the source ties the degradation to sequential, state-dependent reasoning under fixed budgets.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Measured coordination overhead is 58% for Independent, 263% for Decentralized, 285% for Centralized, and 515% for Hybrid, relative to the single-agent baseline.

## Source Excerpt

“Table 5: Coordination metrics across architectures... Overhead (O%) ... SAS 0, Independent 58, Decentralized 263, Centralized 285, Hybrid 515.”

## Note

These values are architecture-level coordination metrics reported in the paper’s summary table.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Total reasoning turns scale with agent count as T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724, with R² = 0.974 and p < 0.001.

## Source Excerpt

“Total reasoning turns ... exhibit power-law growth with agent count: T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724, R2 = 0.974, 95% CI on exponent: [1.685, 1.763], p < 0.001.”

## Note

The source presents this as an empirical scaling relation for turn count and interprets the exponent as evidence of super-linear communication cost growth.
---

---

evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
PlanCraft shows performance degradation for every multi-agent architecture tested, ranging from -39.0% to -70.0% relative to the single-agent baseline.

## Source Excerpt

“Critically, PlanCraft exhibits universal performance degradation across all multi-agent architectures. Centralized declines to -50.4% ... Decentralized to -41.4% ... Hybrid to -39.0% ... and Independent to -70.0%...”

## Note

This is a direct counterexample to uniform multi-agent benefit; the source ties the degradation to sequential, state-dependent reasoning under fixed budgets.
```

---

Total reasoning turns scale with agent count as T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724, with R² = 0.974 and p < 0.001.

## Source Excerpt

“Total reasoning turns ... exhibit power-law growth with agent count: T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724, R2 = 0.974, 95% CI on exponent: [1.685, 1.763], p < 0.001.”

## Note

The source presents this as an empirical scaling relation for turn count and interprets the exponent as evidence of super-linear communication cost growth.

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains controlled empirical evidence about how single-agent and multi-agent LLM systems perform across four agentic benchmarks, including measured effects for architecture, task structure, coordination overhead, error amplification, efficiency, and predictive modeling of architecture-task fit.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study evaluates 180 controlled agent-system configurations spanning 5 architectures, 3 LLM families, and 4 agentic benchmarks with matched prompts, tools, and token budgets.

## Source Excerpt

“We evaluate five canonical architectures: Single Agent System (SAS) and four Multi-Agent variants (Independent, Centralized, Decentralized, Hybrid) instantiated across three major LLM families ... on four representative agentic benchmarks ... Across N = 180 controlled configurations with matched token budgets, we derive a scaling principle...”

## Note

The source frames this design as controlling implementation confounds to isolate architectural effects.
```

## Note

The source states both metrics as evidence of extrapolation to unseen task structures.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-04

citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The efficiency-tools interaction is negative, with β = -0.330 and p < 0.001, indicating stronger performance penalties for multi-agent systems on tool-heavy tasks.

## Source Excerpt

“Our analysis identifies three patterns. First, a tool-coordination trade-off (β = −0.330, p < 0.001): tool-heavy tasks ... suffer from multi-agent coordination overhead...”

## Note

This evidence identifies a quantified interaction and its direction, but the source’s mechanism account remains a proposed explanation tied to fixed computational budgets and tool orchestration capacity.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
When the single-agent baseline exceeds about 45% accuracy, adding coordination shows diminishing or negative returns, with β = -0.408 and p < 0.001.

## Source Excerpt

“Second, a capability ceiling (β = −0.408, p < 0.001): tasks where single-agent performance already exceeds 45% accuracy experience negative returns from additional agents...”

## Note

This introduces a visible tension with generic “more agents help” claims because the reported benefit reverses above an empirical baseline threshold.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Independent multi-agent systems amplify errors by 17.2× relative to the single-agent baseline.

## Source Excerpt

“Third, architecture-dependent error amplification where independent multi-agent systems amplify errors 17.2-fold versus single-agent baseline through unchecked error propagation...”

## Note

This is a strong failure result for one multi-agent topology and conflicts with any assumption that parallel independent agents are automatically safer or more reliable.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Centralized coordination limits error amplification to 4.4× relative to the single-agent baseline by using an orchestrator validation bottleneck before aggregation.

## Source Excerpt

“...while centralized coordination achieves 4.4-fold containment via validation bottlenecks, where the orchestrator reviews sub-agent outputs before aggregation, catching errors before they propagate to the final response.”

## Note

The mechanism is stated directly in the source: orchestrator review acts as the containment point.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---

On Finance Agent, centralized multi-agent coordination improves mean performance by 80.9%, from 0.349 for the single-agent baseline to 0.631.

## Source Excerpt

“On Finance Agent, MAS achieve substantial improvements: Centralized reaches +80.9% (mean 0.631 vs. SAS 0.349)...”

## Note

The source attributes this domain to distributed financial reasoning opportunities, but this evidence unit records only the reported performance change.
```
